title.emphasis: Section III: Statutory and Code-Based Affirmations of Autonomy

section.para: Beyond the foundational protections of state constitutions, the right to bodily autonomy is also explicitly affirmed through various state statutes and legal codes. These laws, while generally less durable than constitutional provisions, provide critical, context-specific protections in the domains of medical decision-making, sexual consent, and reproductive health care. They codify the principle that individuals have the right to control what is done to their bodies and to be free from unwanted physical intrusion.

title.emphasis: The Right to Self-Determination in Medical Consent

section.para: One of the most widespread and fundamental statutory affirmations of bodily autonomy is the legal doctrine of informed consent. This principle, codified in the laws of every state, is based on the right of every individual to determine what shall be done to their own body in the context of medical treatment.27 The doctrine requires a process of communication between a patient and a physician that results in the patient's explicit authorization for a specific medical intervention, thereby giving legal force to the patient's self-determination.28 Analysis of specific state statutes reveals clear language affirming this right:

listitem.para: California: The California Code of Regulations (Tit. 9, § 784.29) provides an unambiguous affirmation of patient control. The text states, "The client has the right to accept or refuse the proposed treatment, and if he or she consents, has the right to revoke his or her consent for any reason at any time".29 This language codifies not only the initial right to decide but also the ongoing right to withdraw consent, reinforcing the principle of continuous personal sovereignty.

New York: The state's Public Health Law (§ 2805-D) frames the right through the lens of malpractice, defining a "lack of informed consent" as the failure to disclose information in a manner "permitting the patient to make a knowledgeable evaluation".30 The statute also explicitly recognizes a patient's right to control the flow of information about their own body by providing a legal defense if "the patient assured the... practitioner that he did not want to be informed".30 This acknowledges the patient's autonomy extends even to the decision of whether to participate in the informed consent process itself.

Texas: The Texas Health and Safety Code (Ch. 313) addresses situations of patient incapacity by establishing a framework for surrogate decision-making. Critically, the law mandates that any decision made by a surrogate "must be based on knowledge of what the patient would desire, if known".31 This provision centers the patient's own values and previously expressed wishes, ensuring that their autonomy is respected to the greatest extent possible even when they cannot communicate for themselves.

Massachusetts: The state's General Laws (Ch. 112, § 12R) require the "written informed consent of the patient seeking an abortion." For minors, the law provides a detailed judicial bypass procedure that respects their autonomy if a judge determines they are mature and capable of giving informed consent, thereby recognizing that age alone is not an absolute barrier to self-determination.32

title.emphasis: The Affirmative Consent Standard in Sexual Assault Law

section.para: A powerful, context-specific affirmation of bodily autonomy is found in the criminal codes of states that have adopted an "affirmative consent" standard for sexual activity. This represents a significant evolution from the historical legal model for sexual assault, which was coercion-based. In a coercion-based framework, the central legal question is whether the perpetrator used "forcible compulsion" to overcome the victim's will.1 This model does not positively affirm a right to be free from unwanted sexual contact in the absence of a specific level of violence.

In contrast, the modern consent-based model, particularly the "only yes means yes" standard of affirmative consent, reframes the crime entirely. It defines sexual assault by the absence of consent, not necessarily the presence of force.1 Affirmative consent is statutorily defined as a knowing, voluntary, and mutual decision, communicated through unambiguous words or actions, to engage in a specific sexual act.1 These statutes explicitly state that silence, passivity, or the absence of physical resistance does not constitute consent.1 This legal standard is a direct affirmation of bodily autonomy because it recognizes each person's default state of self-sovereignty and places the legal responsibility on the person initiating a sexual act to secure positive, unambiguous permission.

This modern legal framework creates a stark contradiction with the standards for non-sexual social interactions. For general social touching, such as incidental contact in a crowded public space, the law relies on the pragmatic, objective standard of "implied consent" to facilitate the functioning of society.33 This "Consent Paradox" reveals that bodily autonomy is not an absolute principle but a context-dependent one. The law grants it the highest level of protection in the sexual sphere, recognizing the unique and profound harms of sexual violation, while significantly lowering that protection in other areas of physical contact for reasons of social utility.

The following table provides a comparative overview of state sexual consent statutes, categorizing them by their legal model and providing the specific statutory definition where available. This illustrates the jurisdictional divide between states that have positively affirmed sexual autonomy through modern consent laws and those that have not.

row.entry: Statutory Definition of Consent (or Lack Thereof)
"Without consent" means the absence of a "freely given, reversible agreement...positively expressed by word or action."
"positive cooperation in act or attitude pursuant to the exercise of free will." Silence or lack of resistance is not consent.
"cooperation in act or attitude pursuant to an exercise of free will and with knowledge of the nature of the act."
No statutory definition of "consent." Lack of consent results from use or threat of force, or incapacity.
"intelligent, knowing, and voluntary consent and does not include coerced submission." Failure to resist is not consent.
"a freely given agreement to the act of sexual penetration or sexual conduct in question."
No statutory definition of "consent." Crime is defined by compelling submission "by force and against his or her will."
"words or overt actions by a person indicating a freely given present agreement to perform a particular sexual act."
"an affirmative, conscious, and voluntary agreement to engage in specific sexual activity."
"Lack of consent" results from forcible compulsion or incapacity, but can be inferred from "impliedly acquiesce."
"actual words or conduct indicating freely given agreement to have sexual intercourse or sexual contact."

title.emphasis: Reproductive Health Acts and Interstate Shield Laws

section.para: In addition to medical and criminal codes, several states have enacted broad statutory schemes that positively affirm reproductive autonomy. These fall short of constitutional amendments but represent significant legislative protections.

listitem.para: Reproductive Health Acts: States including Illinois (Reproductive Health Act of 2019) and New Jersey (Freedom of Reproductive Choice Act of 2022) have passed comprehensive statutes that explicitly declare reproductive health care, including abortion and contraception, to be a "fundamental right".15 These acts codify protections in state law, creating a statutory bulwark against judicial or executive actions that might seek to limit access.

Interstate Shield Laws: A direct response to the post-Dobbs landscape has been the enactment of "shield laws" in numerous states, including New Jersey, Illinois, and California.15 These statutes are designed to protect in-state medical providers, patients who travel for care, and individuals who assist them from out-of-state investigations, subpoenas, and extradition requests related to the provision of reproductive health services that are legal within the state's borders. These laws function as an affirmation of the state's sovereign authority to protect the bodily autonomy of individuals within its jurisdiction from the legal overreach of other states.

section.para: A state like California exemplifies a multi-domain approach to protecting bodily autonomy. It has a post-Dobbs constitutional amendment for reproductive freedom, a pre-existing explicit right to privacy in its constitution that courts have interpreted to protect medical decisions, a robust medical informed consent statute, and a criminal code with a clear affirmative consent standard for sexual assault.1 This layered approach demonstrates that a comprehensive legal framework for bodily autonomy is not monolithic. The strength of the right in any given state is a function of how many of these distinct legal domains contain positive, explicit affirmations of the principle of self-sovereignty.
